title: Learning Git: Version Control, Branches, Merge, Reset and Rebase
excerpt: My personal journey learning Git and understanding how version control helps developers track changes, work safely, collaborate better, and build production-ready software.
content:
# Learning Git: Version Control, Branches, Merge, Reset and Rebase

Today I spent time learning Git, and honestly, it made me understand why Git is one of the most important tools for every developer.

Earlier, I used to think Git was mainly for pushing code to GitHub. But after learning it properly, I realized Git is much more than that.

Git helps us track changes, manage project history, work on features safely, fix mistakes, collaborate with teams, and maintain clean production-ready code.

For a full stack developer, Git is not optional. It is a daily tool.

## What is Git?

Git is a version control system.

In simple words, it keeps track of all important changes in a project.

Without Git, developers may end up creating folders like:

- project-final
- project-final-updated
- project-final-real-final
- project-latest-final

Git solves this problem professionally.

It gives every project a proper history, so we can see what changed, when it changed, who changed it, and why it changed.

## Why Git Matters in Real Projects

When a project is small, managing files manually may look easy.

But once the project grows, things become complex.

There may be multiple features, bugs, experiments, UI changes, backend changes, and urgent fixes happening together.

Git helps developers manage all of this without breaking the main codebase.

Some key benefits of Git are:

- Tracking code history
- Working on multiple features safely
- Creating separate branches
- Fixing mistakes
- Handling merge conflicts
- Collaborating with other developers
- Reviewing old commits
- Keeping production code stable

This is why almost every modern software team uses Git.

## Git Initialization

The first step is initializing Git inside a project.

```bash
git init
```

This command creates a local Git repository.

After this, Git starts tracking the project files and changes.

It feels like giving the project a proper memory system.

## Staging and Commit

Git has a clean workflow:

```bash
git add .
git commit -m "add initial project setup"
```

`git add` moves changes to the staging area.

`git commit` saves those staged changes as a snapshot.

A commit should always have a meaningful message.

Bad commit message:

```bash
git commit -m "changes"
```

Better commit message:

```bash
git commit -m "add responsive portfolio navbar"
```

Good commit messages make project history easier to understand.

## Git Log and Commit Hash

The `git log` command shows commit history.

```bash
git log
```

Every commit has a unique hash.

That hash helps us identify a specific point in project history.

This is useful when debugging, reverting, checking old code, or understanding how a feature evolved over time.

## Branching in Git

Branching is one of the most powerful Git concepts.

A branch allows us to work on a feature separately without disturbing the main branch.

Example:

```bash
git checkout -b feature/blog-page
```

This creates a new branch for the blog page.

Now I can build, test, and experiment safely.

The main branch remains stable.

This is very useful in professional projects where multiple features are developed at the same time.

## Merge in Git

Once a feature is complete, it can be merged back into the main branch.

```bash
git checkout main
git merge feature/blog-page
```

Merge combines changes from one branch into another.

This is useful when a feature is complete and ready to become part of the main codebase.

## Merge Conflicts

Merge conflicts happen when Git cannot automatically decide which code to keep.

This usually happens when two branches change the same part of the same file.

At first, conflicts can look scary. But Git is simply asking the developer to manually decide the correct final code.

The process is:

- Open the conflicted file
- Check both versions
- Keep the correct code
- Remove conflict markers
- Stage the resolved file
- Commit the fix

Merge conflicts taught me that Git is not only about commands. It is also about understanding code changes properly.

## Git Stash

`git stash` is useful when we are working on something but need to switch branches quickly.

```bash
git stash
```

This temporarily saves uncommitted changes.

Later, we can bring them back using:

```bash
git stash pop
```

This is very practical when an urgent bug fix comes in while we are working on an incomplete feature.

## Git Reset

Git reset is used to move back in history or undo commits.

There are three common types:

```bash
git reset --soft HEAD~1
```

This removes the last commit but keeps changes staged.

```bash
git reset --mixed HEAD~1
```

This removes the last commit and unstages the changes.

```bash
git reset --hard HEAD~1
```

This removes the last commit and deletes the changes from the working directory.

The most important lesson is that `git reset --hard` should be used carefully because it can remove local changes permanently.

## Git Rebase

Rebase is one of the advanced Git concepts I learned.

In simple words, rebase moves commits from one branch and places them on top of another branch.

Example:

```bash
git checkout feature/blog-page
git rebase main
```

This makes the feature branch look like it was created from the latest version of main.

Rebase helps create a cleaner and more linear commit history.

## Merge vs Rebase

Merge and rebase both combine changes, but they work differently.

Merge preserves history exactly as it happened.

Rebase rewrites commit history to make it cleaner and linear.

Use merge when you want to preserve branch history.

Use rebase when you want a cleaner commit history before merging.

A simple way to remember:

- Merge = combine branches safely
- Rebase = replay commits cleanly

## Git Reset vs Rebase

Reset and rebase are also different.

Reset is mainly used to undo commits or move the branch pointer backward.

Rebase is used to move commits on top of another branch.

A simple comparison:

- Reset changes where your branch points
- Rebase changes the base of your commits

Both are powerful, but both should be used carefully.

## Pushing Code to GitHub

After committing locally, code can be pushed to GitHub.

```bash
git push origin main
```

GitHub makes the code available online and helps with collaboration, pull requests, code reviews, and deployment workflows.

For portfolio projects, GitHub also acts as proof of work.

It shows consistency, project structure, commit history, and real development activity.

## Forking

Forking is useful when we want to create our own copy of someone else's repository.

This is mostly used in open-source contribution.

The common flow is:

- Fork the repository
- Clone it locally
- Create a new branch
- Make changes
- Push changes
- Open a pull request

This helps developers contribute to projects without directly changing the original repository.

## Key Lessons I Learned

After learning Git, I understood that Git is not just a command-line tool.

It teaches discipline.

It teaches developers to work in a structured way.

Some of my biggest takeaways are:

- Commit small and meaningful changes
- Write clear commit messages
- Use branches for features
- Keep main branch stable
- Understand merge conflicts instead of fearing them
- Use reset carefully
- Use rebase for cleaner history
- Push code regularly

## Final Thoughts

Learning Git gave me more confidence as a developer.

Now I understand how professional teams manage code, collaborate, review changes, and ship features safely.

For my personal portfolio and future projects, Git will help me maintain cleaner code, better history, and a more professional development workflow.

Git is not just about saving code.

It is about building software with confidence, structure, and control.

And that is why every serious developer should learn Git properly.
